presentation:
A 58-year-old woman was admitted to this hospital for vision loss in the right eye, acute progressive vision loss in the left eye, headaches, and rashes.

Four years before admission, a brown, nonpainful, nonpruritic, maculopapular rash developed on the trunk. Approximately 4 weeks later, the rash had spontaneously resolved; however, painful, peeling, erythematous papules had appeared on the hands and in the web spaces between the fingers, and oral and nasal aphthous ulcerations had occurred. During the following weeks, the hand lesions and nasal ulcers spontaneously resolved. The oral ulcers waxed and waned but continued to be present intermittently.

Three and a half years before admission, bilateral temporal pingueculae were identified on routine ophthalmologic examination. Approximately 18 months later, pain and redness developed in both eyes; the patient received a diagnosis of bilateral conjunctival ulcerations, and prednisolone eyedrops were prescribed. Four weeks later, she returned with headaches on the right side and 3 days of bilateral blurry vision, photophobia that led her to wear sunglasses indoors, and redness and pain in the right eye. Examination revealed scleritis in the right eye and oral ulcers; prednisolone eyedrops were resumed, and oral piroxicam was prescribed.

Twenty-one months before admission, the patient was evaluated in the rheumatology clinic for worsened oral ulcers and recurrent blurry vision in the left eye that had developed as the glucocorticoid dose was tapered. Examination was notable for aphthous ulcers on the tongue and several nasal erosions. Tests for ANA, rheumatoid factor, ANCA, and anti-CCP were negative.

One month later, she returned with pain in the left eye (rated 7 out of 10) and recurrent blurry vision and photophobia. Examination revealed new scleritis in the left eye.

During the next 6 months, the patient could not be fully weaned off glucocorticoid therapy because of chronic asthma and worsening oral ulcerations. Azathioprine, adalimumab, and intravitreal glucocorticoids were administered for scleritis and oral ulcers. Because testing for HLA-B51 was positive, a working diagnosis of atypical Behcet's disease was considered. During the next 2 months, two doses of rituximab were administered for persistent eye pain, blurry vision, and photophobia.

During the next 10 months, the patient had monthly follow-up for persistent pain in both eyes and waxing and waning redness, blurry vision, and photophobia. Treatments included pulse therapy with high-dose glucocorticoids, two additional doses of rituximab with cyclophosphamide, and cyclosporine (which caused worsening leg rashes and ulcerations). While receiving rituximab, CD4+CD3+ T cells were 46 per cubic millimeter (reference range, 419 to 1,590) and CD8+CD3+ T cells were 97 per cubic millimeter (reference range, 190 to 1,140). The percentage of total lymphocytes was 3%, and no B cells were detected.

Two months before admission, ophthalmologic examination revealed new bilateral iridocyclitis; loteprednol eyedrops and tocilizumab were administered, and the iridocyclitis resolved.

Four weeks later, the patient noticed decreased vision in her right eye. Examination revealed new panuveitis with retinitis. Intravitreal dexamethasone implant was placed, and infliximab was administered. One week later, there was new retinitis in the inferior aspect of the right eye.

Three days later (6 days before admission), the patient had decreased vision in the left eye and worsened headaches in a bifrontal distribution, with tearing, nasal pain, and nausea. A painful desquamating rash began on her lower legs and spread proximally, accompanied by worsened painful peeling of her hands. Two days before admission, she had decreased vision in both eyes with photophobia and shooting eye pain.

The patient had a 20-year history of asthma with multiple hospitalizations. She had had spontaneous pneumothoraxes in her 20s, GERD, breast fibroadenoma, and hypertension. Her ocular history included meibomian gland dysfunction and aqueous tear deficiency, and she had undergone bilateral LASIK 12 years earlier. A review of systems was notable for chronic headaches, pain in the knees and elbows, mild epistaxis, and Raynaud's phenomenon. She had no fever, chills, anorexia, weight loss, confusion, seizure, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing loss, hair loss, jaw claudication, history of genital ulcers, or GI/GU symptoms.

Medications were prednisone, atovaquone, pantoprazole, theophylline, and valganciclovir. She taught at an academic institution in New England, drank wine once weekly, was divorced and lived with her boyfriend (monogamous), and had a dog. She had traveled to Nevada (caught in a dust storm) and Shanghai. Her father had Crohn's disease, her mother had glaucoma and breast cancer, one sister had Hashimoto's thyroiditis, and the other had endometrial cancer.

On examination, temperature was 36.4°C, heart rate 98, blood pressure 157/99 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20, oxygen saturation 97% on ambient air. BMI was 21.3. The sclerae were erythematous, and the right pupil was nonreactive to light. There were two beats of clonus on dorsiflexion of the ankles. Multiple aphthous ulcers were present on the buccal mucosa, inner surfaces of the lips, and linear ulcers on the tongue edges. There was an erythematous rash on the palms with desquamation, healing fissures in the web spaces between fingers, and tender erythematous papules on both upper arms. There was an erythematous maculopapular rash on the legs with scaled and scabbed lesions and areas of epidermal atrophy. Bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy was present. No joint erythema, tenderness, or warmth was noted.

CT of the head was normal. MRI of the head including venography and arteriography showed no acute intracranial or intraocular abnormality. Mild dilatation of the superior ophthalmic veins was noted, greater on the right side.

Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual acuity of 20/25 in the left eye, only light perception in the right eye, and an afferent pupillary defect in the right eye. Slit-lamp examination revealed bilateral scleral thinning, nuclear sclerosis, anterior chamber cells 2+, vitreous cells 3+ and haze 2+ in the right eye. Funduscopic examination revealed round, circumferential, yellow retinal lesions consistent with infection. Intravitreal foscarnet and ganciclovir were administered, and oral valacyclovir was started.

Lumbar puncture showed opening pressure 22 cm H2O, colorless CSF, glucose 60 mg/dl (reference 50-75), total protein 160 mg/dl (reference 5-55), 64 nucleated cells/mm3 (reference 0-5) with 63% neutrophils, 16% lymphocytes, 21% monocytes. Punch biopsy of the leg rash was performed.

Blood tests were negative for toxoplasma antibodies and for CMV DNA and VZV DNA. CSF tests were negative for cryptococcal antigen and for HHV-6 DNA, HSV types 1 and 2 DNA, CMV DNA, and VZV DNA. Vitreous fluid tests were negative for toxoplasma and for HSV DNA, CMV DNA, and VZV DNA.

presentation:
A 69-year-old man was evaluated in the pulmonary clinic of this hospital because of progressive dyspnea.

Ten years before this evaluation, the patient noticed labored breathing with activity, initially while climbing stairs and then while walking on level ground. He was evaluated with a treadmill stress test and transthoracic echocardiography; the results of both were reportedly normal. The dyspnea abated.

Thirty months before this evaluation, fatigue, malaise, sore throat, rhinorrhea, and arthralgias developed. A chest radiograph showed moderately inflated lungs, with subcentimeter, faint nodular opacities in the upper lung zones. Azithromycin was prescribed.

CT of the chest revealed diffuse subpleural reticulation, without honeycombing or traction bronchiectasis or bronchiolectasis. The patient had a few bilateral, scattered, solid pulmonary nodules measuring 3 to 6 mm. There was mild enlargement of a low right paratracheal lymph node (11 mm) and of a right hilar lymph node (14 mm), and aortic and coronary atherosclerosis was present.

Seventeen months before this evaluation, the patient had a several-week history of rhinorrhea and a cough intermittently productive of green sputum. A radiograph of the sinuses showed opacification of the right frontal sinus but no evidence of sinusitis. Twelve months before this evaluation, he reported insidious onset of exertional dyspnea, first noticed while climbing several flights of stairs. The dyspnea had progressed and occurred with less exertion. He traveled to Denver and felt increased dyspnea at altitude.

Six months before this evaluation, a nuclear stress test with myocardial perfusion imaging revealed a small area of distal anterior ischemia. Three months later, transthoracic echocardiography showed LVEF of 70%, mild concentric LVH, mild aortic dilatation, and no evidence of valvular disease; the right ventricular systolic pressure could not be estimated.

Four weeks before this evaluation, he was seen for ongoing dyspnea with any amount of exertion and cough. Pulmonary CT angiography revealed persistence of diffuse subpleural reticular opacities involving all lobes without distinct apical or basal predominance. There was new bilateral traction bronchiolectasis, new dilatation of the main pulmonary artery (33 mm), and increased enlargement of mediastinal lymph nodes (including a low right paratracheal node at 17 mm and right hilar node at 17 mm). There were new ill-defined ground-glass opacities in both lungs (likely due to low lung volumes and expiratory phase imaging). There were a few lobular areas of hypoattenuating parenchyma suggestive of mild air trapping. There was no evidence of pulmonary embolism.

Two weeks before this evaluation, pulmonary-function testing revealed FEV1 2.50 liters (82% predicted), FVC 2.90 liters (73% predicted), FEV1/FVC ratio 0.86, total lung capacity 3.92 liters (61% predicted), and DLCO (corrected for hemoglobin) 45% predicted. These results were interpreted as indicative of a restrictive ventilatory deficit with diffusion impairment.

On evaluation, the patient reported ongoing dyspnea provoked by any amount of exertion and an occasional morning cough productive of scant greenish-brown mucus. He reported no fever, weight loss, hemoptysis, chest pain, wheezing, weakness, fatigue, dry eyes, dry mouth, heartburn, dysphagia, rash, Raynaud's phenomenon, edema, myalgias, or arthralgias.

Medical history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary-artery calcifications, right bundle-branch block, osteoarthritis, prostatic hypertrophy, and GERD. He had undergone right carotid endarterectomy, right total knee replacement, removal of a tubular adenoma of the colon, and Mohs surgery for multiple basal-cell carcinomas. Medications included aspirin, rosuvastatin, and omeprazole. Exposure to cats and wool caused watery eyes.

He had smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 10 years and quit more than 40 years earlier; he had smoked five cigars per week for 15 years and quit 5 years earlier. He drank one serving of liquor daily. He lived in coastal Massachusetts and worked in marketing and consulting with no known occupational or inhalational exposures. He had no pets or exposure to molds. His father had died of MI at 54; there was no family history of lung or autoimmune disease.

The temperature was 36.8°C, heart rate 68, blood pressure 134/78 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18, oxygen saturation 96% at rest and 95% after climbing one flight of stairs. Auscultation revealed bilateral crackles at end inspiration, most prominent over the right lung base. The remainder of the examination was normal.

presentation:
A 55-year-old man with a history of opioid use disorder and hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection presented to this hospital with jaundice.

Four months before the current presentation, the patient was released from prison after a 2-year incarceration. After he left prison, he resumed injecting heroin and had three episodes of overdose. He was evaluated at another hospital for symptoms of depression and was admitted for psychiatric treatment. During that admission, sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone therapy was initiated, and the patient was discharged.

One day after discharge and 5 weeks before the current presentation, headache, body aches, sweats, diarrhea, and nausea developed. The patient presented to a clinic for substance use disorder. He reported that he had been unable to obtain sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone from a pharmacy after discharge. He appeared restless, but the remainder of the physical examination was normal. Urine toxicologic screening was positive for buprenorphine, norbuprenorphine, and norfentanyl; buprenorphine-naloxone therapy was resumed.

Three weeks before the current presentation, dark urine and light-headedness developed and did not improve with increased fluid consumption. The patient also noticed slow thinking and arthralgias in the hands, wrists, and elbows. He was evaluated by a new primary care provider. The joints of the hands, wrists, and elbows were normal. The neurologic examination was normal. Blood levels of electrolytes and glucose were normal, as were the complete blood count, differential count, and results of renal-function tests. Urinalysis revealed clear, dark-yellow urine, with specific gravity 1.020, pH 7.5, trace ketones, and 2+ urobilinogen.

One week before the current presentation, the patient noticed yellowing of the eyes and skin. One week later, he was seen by his primary care provider and was immediately transported to the emergency department of this hospital.

On evaluation, the patient reported 2 weeks of anorexia, malaise, nausea with dark-brown emesis, profuse nonbloody watery diarrhea, intermittent abdominal cramping, poor sleep, blurry vision, and episodes of forgetfulness and loss of concentration. He had no fevers, chills, bleeding, or pulmonary or genitourinary symptoms and had no sick contacts. There was a history of osteoarthritis, depression, and anxiety. HCV infection had been diagnosed but not treated while the patient was incarcerated. He reported that when he used intravenous heroin, he did not reuse, share, or lick needles. Allergies included penicillin, aspirin, and sulfonamide-containing antibiotics. The only medication was sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone.

The patient was divorced and had no children. He had not been sexually active in 2 years. He was born in the northeastern United States and had not traveled outside the country. Since his release from prison, he was unemployed, homeless, and sleeping in a shelter. He had no known exposure to rodents or rodent excreta. He smoked cigarettes daily for 20 years. He had previously consumed 2 liters of vodka daily but had quit 6 years earlier. His parents were deceased; he was estranged from his siblings.

On physical examination, the temperature was 36.3°C, the pulse 57 beats per minute, the blood pressure 113/72 mm Hg, the respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and the oxygen saturation 97% while breathing ambient air. The weight was 68 kg. The patient appeared jaundiced with marked scleral icterus. The mucous membranes were moist, and there was no cervical lymphadenopathy. The abdomen was soft and nondistended, with no evidence of ascites. There was mild tenderness on palpation of the upper right quadrant, with no hepatosplenomegaly. He had no gynecomastia, caput medusae, or spider angiomas. He was alert and fully oriented with no asterixis. There was no rash or leg edema.

Blood levels of electrolytes, glucose, and lipase were normal, as were the complete blood count, differential count, and results of renal-function tests. Serum toxicologic screening did not detect acetaminophen, salicylates, tricyclics, or ethanol.

Limited ultrasonography of the right upper quadrant revealed no bile-duct dilatation, a patent main portal vein with hepatopetal flow, and normal hepatic parenchymal echotexture. Diffuse, hypoechoic gallbladder-wall thickening was present, without gallbladder distention, cholelithiasis, or pericholecystic fluid. Murphy's sign was negative. There was trace perihepatic ascites.

presentation:
A 44-year-old man was admitted to this hospital because of joint pain, unintentional weight loss, and chest pain.

Ten months before admission, arthralgias developed in the shoulders. After 2 months, MRI of the shoulders was reportedly normal. Piroxicam was prescribed, and glucocorticoid injections were administered. During the next 4 months, arthralgias developed in the fingers and knees. A diagnosis of fibromyalgia was made. Naproxen was prescribed, and additional glucocorticoid injections to the shoulders were administered.

Six weeks before admission, anorexia, odynophagia, and epigastric pain developed. Two weeks later, episodic anterior pleuritic pain developed, which was sharp and worse in the supine position; he also had intermittent dyspnea and nonproductive cough. He had fatigue sometimes severe enough to prevent him from getting out of bed. One week before admission, subjective fevers and increased dyspnea occurred.

One day before admission, the patient appeared weak and ill and was taken to the emergency department of another hospital. The white-cell count was 2,000 per cubic millimeter and the hematocrit was 21%. A chest radiograph showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette, small bilateral pleural effusions, and mild interstitial pulmonary edema. CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed small-volume ascites with peritoneal enhancement, a moderate-to-large circumferential pericardial effusion, and small bilateral pleural effusions. He was transferred to this hospital.

A review of systems was notable for fatigue, mild bilateral joint pain, pleuritic pain, intermittent dyspnea, nonproductive cough, anorexia, odynophagia, an unintentional weight loss of 18 kg during the past year, polyuria, and a pruritic rash on the arms with some sloughing that spared the hands. He had no night sweats, chills, melena, hematochezia, diarrhea, photosensitivity, dry eyes or mouth, Raynaud's phenomenon, or edema.

The patient's medical history was notable for malaria and depression. Medications included bupropion and naproxen as needed. He was born and raised in sub-Saharan Africa and had immigrated to New England 18 years earlier; he had not traveled outside the United States in 15 years. He was divorced and had been sexually active with women, most recently 7 months before presentation. He lived with his sister and two young children. He had had a 7-year period of heavy beer drinking but had been abstinent for almost 20 years; he had smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for 12 years but quit 15 years earlier. No illicit drug use. No family history of heart disease, HIV, tuberculosis, autoimmune disease, or cancer.

On examination, temperature was 37.7°C, heart rate 100, blood pressure 93/57 mm Hg (pulsus paradoxus 12 mm Hg), respiratory rate 18, oxygen saturation 96% on ambient air. BMI was 18.1. The patient had cachexia and bitemporal wasting. Jugular venous pressure was 14 cm of water. He had a yellow coating on the tongue but no oral lesions. He had scaling and dry skin on the arms and chest but no overt rash. Diffuse subcentimeter cervical lymph nodes were palpable, as were larger bilateral axillary lymph nodes. The heart rhythm was tachycardic and distant, and the lungs were clear. There was mild epigastric tenderness. Shoulder abduction was limited to 90 degrees bilaterally with pain on external rotation. The knees were warm with moderate effusions and pain on flexion. He had synovitis and tenderness of the wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints.

Urinalysis showed 1+ urobilinogen, 2+ protein, 0-2 erythrocytes/HPF, and 3-5 leukocytes/HPF. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm at 96 bpm, subtle PR-segment depression, and borderline low limb-lead voltage. Echocardiography showed normal LV size and function with a moderate-sized pericardial effusion with diffuse fibrin deposition and brief RV diastolic inversion.

On the second day, temperature reached 39.1°C. Blood cultures were obtained. Screening tests for HIV types 1 and 2 and p24 antigen, Lyme disease antibodies, and heterophile antibodies were negative. Serologic testing for CMV IgG was positive. A PPD was planted.

On the third day, temperature reached 39.7°C. Nonsustained ventricular tachycardia occurred. CT of the chest showed enlargement of bilateral pleural effusions and pericardial effusion, with enlarged bilateral axillary lymph nodes.

On the fourth day, repeat echocardiography showed a large circumferential pericardial effusion (3 cm) causing diastolic inversion of RV free wall and right atrium, with 40% respirophasic variation in mitral valve Doppler velocities and new IVC dilatation — consistent with cardiac tamponade. Pericardiocentesis retrieved 570 ml of bloody fluid.

Pericardial fluid: abundant neutrophils, no organisms on Gram stain, glucose 77 mg/dl, LDH 1,487 U/L, total protein 4.0 g/dl, red-cell count 18,000/mm3, nucleated-cell count 9,439/mm3 (93% neutrophils). Cytology negative for malignant cells. Interferon-gamma release assay for TB negative. Tests for rheumatoid factor, antitreponemal antibodies, hepatitis B antibodies, and parvovirus IgM were negative.

presentation:
An 83-year-old woman was admitted to the hospital because of severe upper-back pain, hypertension, and anuria.

There was a history of hypertension that dated back 34 years and was treated in recent years with propranolol, atenolol, and hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene at various times. She had smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily for an unknown number of years. Ten years before admission the patient began to have transient cerebral ischemic attacks characterized by weakness and numbness of the left hand and arm. Two years thereafter a right cerebrovascular accident was treated at another hospital, with a satisfactory recovery.

Three months later she was admitted to this hospital because of right carotid stenosis. Angiography showed critical stenosis at the origin of the right internal carotid artery with functional occlusion; severe stenosis also involved the right external carotid artery; the left carotid and vertebral arteries appeared normal. A CT cranial scan revealed a right cerebrovascular accident weeks to a few months old. A right carotid endarterectomy with saphenous-vein patch angioplasty was performed without complications.

Three and a half years before entry severe headaches developed, and a neurologic consultant found that the left temporal artery was tender and indurated; the erythrocyte sedimentation rate was considerably elevated. Biopsy of the temporal artery revealed giant-cell arteritis. Prednisone (60 mg daily) was begun. The headaches disappeared; the dose was gradually tapered but was not discontinued.

Twenty-two months before admission a urinary tract infection developed with bacteremia due to E. coli, which resolved after intravenous antibiotics. Mild adult-onset diabetes mellitus was discovered and managed with diet alone. Ten months before entry lumbar pain occurred with right-sided sciatica. Radiographs showed degenerative joint disease of the spine and osteoarthritis of both hips. MRI of the lumbar spine revealed two herniated lumbar disks. The pain gradually responded to conservative measures.

Eight months before admission antihypertensive medications were discontinued because of several bouts of nausea and vomiting with dehydration and hypotension. The ESR was 32 mm per hour, and prednisone (5 mg daily) was continued with supplemental potassium chloride. Forty-five days before entry she had an episode of syncope lasting several minutes while seated at the table. Blood pressure was 200/90 mm Hg. She was still taking prednisone 5 mg daily.

One day before admission severe, "tearing" dorsal pain developed abruptly, accompanied by sweating, dizziness, and near syncope; no anterior chest pain or dyspnea was experienced. At another hospital, blood pressure was 210/140 mm Hg and pulse 120. The urine was normal; coagulation studies were negative; creatinine was 1.4 mg per 100 ml, and electrolytes were normal. An electrocardiogram was unchanged from an earlier tracing. Chest X-ray showed bilateral widening of the mediastinum, with a lobulated mass component in the extreme left apex; a small left pleural effusion was evident. The patient was admitted to an intensive care unit where morphine sulfate, propranolol, and metoprolol were administered intravenously with several liters of fluids. Blood pressure fell to 140/70 mm Hg. On the morning of the second hospital day anuria developed, and the patient was transferred to this hospital.

The temperature was 35.6 degrees C, the pulse was 80, and the respirations were 16. The blood pressure was 120/60 mm Hg. On physical examination the patient was pale and frail and in mild discomfort. The carotid pulses were equal, without bruits. The lungs were clear except for diminished breath sounds at the left base. The heart was normal; no murmur or S3 was heard. The abdomen was soft, with faint bowel sounds; no abdominal bruit was heard, and no organs or masses were felt. The peripheral pulses were symmetric and full, except that the posterior tibial pulses were diminished bilaterally; there was no peripheral edema or cyanosis. Neurologic examination was negative.

presentation:
A 28-year-old woman was seen in the emergency department of this hospital because of headache, fever, and a rash.

The patient had been well until the morning of the day of admission, when she awoke with a severe generalized headache. The headache worsened with movement, was not relieved by acetaminophen or ibuprofen, and was accompanied by nausea and one episode of nonbloody, nonbilious emesis. The patient returned to sleep and awoke again several hours later with diffuse myalgias; her temperature was 37.7°C. At that time, she also noted a rash on her abdomen, chest, and right arm; the lesions were small, red, flat, nonpruritic, and nonpainful. Her father, a physician, advised her to go to the emergency department.

On arrival, the patient reported that nausea had resolved and that mild neck stiffness had developed. She had had bacterial meningitis at 9 years of age, with symptoms similar to those of the current illness. She had no history of otitis media, sinusitis, pneumonia, or skin infections. She had a remote history of hospitalization after a motor vehicle accident. Ten months before the current illness, she had had an episode of nephrolithiasis with spontaneous passage of a renal stone. She also had a history of anxiety. Her medications were citalopram and a combined estrogen-progestin oral contraceptive. Immunizations were up to date and reportedly included vaccination against Neisseria meningitidis. She lived alone, worked as a lawyer, and had not recently traveled outside New England or had exposure to sick persons. She had a new boyfriend but was not currently sexually active. She did not smoke or use illicit drugs and rarely consumed alcohol. She had contact with her parents' two dogs but had not had any recent tick bites, mosquito bites, or exposure to other animals. No family history of immunodeficiency or autoimmune disease.

On examination, the patient was alert and oriented. Temperature was 37.3°C, pulse 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 122/68 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 100% on ambient air. She had scattered petechiae on the chest, abdomen, and thighs and a purpuric lesion on the right shoulder that was tender on palpation without crepitus. The remainder of the examination was normal.

Tests for influenza virus and respiratory syncytial virus were negative. A lumbar puncture was performed, and ceftriaxone, vancomycin, acyclovir, and dexamethasone were administered intravenously.

Within 90 minutes after arrival, the temperature rose to 39.0°C and pulse increased to 122 beats per minute. Over the next 3 hours, additional petechiae were noted on her trunk, arms, and legs. The activated partial-thromboplastin time and levels of fibrinogen, haptoglobin, and lactate dehydrogenase were normal; the D-dimer level was 785 ng per milliliter (reference range, <500).

During the first hospital day, she continued to have a headache and was noted to have mild nuchal rigidity, but fever and tachycardia resolved and myalgias decreased.

presentation:
A 60-year-old woman was seen in the emergency department at this hospital after a syncopal episode.

The patient had been well until the day of admission, when light-headedness and dyspnea suddenly developed while she was walking with her husband. The dyspnea improved rapidly with rest, but when she attempted to walk again, she lost consciousness and was lowered to the ground by her husband. She rapidly regained consciousness, and her husband called emergency medical services. On evaluation by paramedical personnel, the oxygen saturation was 71% while she was breathing ambient air and increased to 99% while she was breathing oxygen through a nonrebreather face mask. She was brought to the emergency department by ambulance.

On evaluation, the patient reported feeling well, with no shortness of breath, chest pain, light-headedness, dizziness, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea; she had had no similar episodes in the past. She reported having a transient episode of cramping in the left calf on the morning of admission that had resolved spontaneously. She had no other notable information in her medical history. She took no medications and had no known allergies. She lived with her husband, had no children, and was a retired health care worker. She did not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. She had traveled approximately 2 weeks earlier by airplane for 4 hours. Her father had died of a stroke at the age of 58 years, and her mother had hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and heart failure. There was no other family history of thrombotic or bleeding events or sudden death.

On examination, the patient appeared well and was not in distress. The blood pressure was 121/81 mm Hg, the pulse 77 beats per minute, the respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, and the oxygen saturation 88% while she was breathing ambient air and 98% while she was breathing oxygen through a nasal cannula at a rate of 6 liters per minute; the temperature was normal. The weight was 104.6 kg, and the abdomen was obese; the remainder of the examination was normal. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm at a rate of 74 beats per minute, as well as incomplete right bundle-branch block, diffuse submillimeter ST-segment depressions, and T-wave inversions.

presentation:
A 38-year-old woman was admitted to this hospital because of anemia and thrombocytopenia.

The patient had been well until approximately 3 months before admission, when fatigue, malaise, and light-headedness developed that she attributed to a viral illness. Three weeks before admission, fatigue worsened, and dyspnea on exertion, bruising on the legs, dark urine, and headache developed. She reported that she "felt like staying in bed all day" and needed assistance with showering. Two days before admission, she went to another hospital. The physical examination was reportedly normal, as were the results of coagulation tests. Screening for HIV antibodies and a rapid plasma reagin test were negative.

Examination of the peripheral-blood smear reportedly revealed normal white cell morphology, reduced platelet count, spherocytes, and polychromatic red cells. A direct antiglobulin test (Coombs' test) was positive, and warm-reacting and cold-reacting autoantibodies were reportedly present. Results of serum and urinary protein electrophoresis were normal. Glucocorticoids (methylprednisolone 150 mg twice daily for 2 days, followed by prednisone 70 mg twice daily) were administered, but the patient's condition did not improve. CT scans of the abdomen and pelvis were normal. On the second night, hypotension developed but improved after intravenous fluids. On the third day, the hematocrit was 16.8%. One unit of packed red cells was transfused. The patient was transferred to this hospital because of difficulty finding additional compatible red-cell units.

On admission, the patient reported a weight loss of 2.3 kg during the previous week, intermittent blurred or double vision in both eyes, and for the previous 3 months, transient vesicular lesions on the thighs that resolved spontaneously within 2 or 3 days. She did not have hematuria, dysuria, chest pain, abdominal pain, fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, arthralgias, myalgias, melena, or hematochezia. Approximately 3 years earlier, she had had a similar illness with dyspnea, light-headedness, and an unintentional weight loss of 18 kg, which had gradually resolved spontaneously. She had a history of depression, anemia, and during childhood and pregnancy, epistaxis; she had had a hysterectomy at 22 years of age because of metrorrhagia after a cesarean section. Medications included bupropion, a multivitamin, and occasionally flaxseeds. She was born in South America, moved to the United States more than 10 years earlier, and worked with children. She had recently traveled to Central America and the Caribbean. She did not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Her father had hyperthyroidism, and her mother had diabetes mellitus.

On examination, blood pressure was 105/55 mm Hg, and other vital signs and oxygen saturation were normal. She had mild conjunctival icterus and a single vesicular lesion (1 cm) with an erythematous base on the posterior right thigh. The remainder of the examination was normal. Red-cell indexes were normal, as were prothrombin time, INR, renal-function tests, electrolytes, phosphorus, magnesium, calcium, total protein, albumin, globulin, alkaline phosphatase, CRP, lipase, amylase, folate, and vitamin B12. Testing for lupus anticoagulant was negative. Peripheral-blood smear revealed macrocytes, polychromatocytes, microspherocytes, very few metamyelocytes, predominantly normal platelets, and no schistocytes or malignant cells. Urinalysis revealed 3+ urobilinogen and was otherwise normal. Blood type O, Rh-positive; indirect and direct antiglobulin tests were positive.

Transfusion of 1 unit of leukoreduced red cells was attempted but pain and burning developed immediately at the infusion site. A second attempt on the contralateral side caused pain, burning, facial flushing, and a drop in systolic blood pressure from 94 to 84 mm Hg within 15 minutes. The transfusion was stopped and symptoms resolved. Culture of the thigh lesion revealed herpes simplex virus type 2; blood cultures were sterile.

presentation:
A 54-year-old woman was admitted to this hospital because of abdominal pain, vomiting, and confusion.

The patient was in her usual health until approximately 3 days before admission, when she began to feel unwell, with weakness, chills, and skin abnormally warm to the touch. She self-administered aspirin without improvement. During the next 2 days, her oral intake decreased. Approximately 22 hours before presentation, vomiting occurred. Nine hours before presentation, she began to travel home to Italy from the eastern United States. During the next 2 hours, increasing abdominal pain occurred, associated with vomiting and shortness of breath, and she took additional aspirin for pain. Approximately 2 hours before presentation, while in flight, abdominal pain markedly worsened, vomiting increased, and she became confused and unresponsive. The flight was diverted to Boston.

On examination by EMS, she was nonverbal and moaning continuously. Blood pressure was 120/70 mm Hg, pulse 52 beats per minute, respiratory rate 26 breaths per minute. Capillary blood glucose was 116 mg/dl.

History was obtained from her husband through an interpreter. She had type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, nephrolithiasis, and chronic kidney disease. Medications included enalapril, metformin, glimepiride, nimesulide, imipramine, aspirin, and ibuprofen. She lived in Italy and had vacationed in North America for 10 days, traveling to urban areas. She did not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs, and there was no history of unusual ingestions.

On examination, the patient was incoherent, agitated, and uncomfortable with frequent groaning. She was oriented to person only. Blood pressure was 120/70 mm Hg, pulse 52, temperature 36.7°C, respiratory rate 18, oxygen saturation 95% on ambient air. Pupils were 3 mm and minimally reactive to light; oral mucous membranes were dry; neck was supple. Abdomen was soft without distention, rebound, or guarding. Skin was cool. On neurologic examination, she withdrew all extremities to pain; cranial nerves and strength appeared normal. An electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation at a rate of 115 with a QRS of 94 msec, with a tremulous baseline possibly obscuring ST-segment depression in the inferior leads.

Within 2 hours, tachypnea and increasing somnolence developed. The trachea was intubated. A chest radiograph showed no evidence of pneumonia or pleural effusion.

Approximately 3 hours after arrival, the rectal temperature decreased to 31.7°C and blood pressure fell to 84/43 mm Hg. Norepinephrine, bicarbonate, and warmed fluids were administered. Dark-brown gastric secretions positive for occult blood were aspirated through an orogastric tube; gastric pH was 5.7.

CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed pancreatic edema, peripancreatic fat stranding, a small amount of perihepatic and pericholecystic fluid without biliary ductal dilatation, thickened walls in several loops of small bowel, and an atrophic left kidney containing a nonobstructing calculus. CT of the chest revealed dependent atelectasis only. CT of the brain was normal.

Cefepime, vancomycin, and metronidazole were administered. Toxicologic screening of blood and urine was negative. She was admitted to the cardiac ICU. Continuous venovenous hemofiltration with bicarbonate solution was begun 8 hours after presentation. Echocardiography revealed normal global cardiac function without pericardial effusion. Ultrasonography showed small-volume ascites, nonspecific gallbladder wall thickening, an atrophic left kidney, and increased renal parenchymal echogenicity bilaterally.

During the first 17 hours, the patient had oliguria with approximately 125 ml of urine excreted.

presentation:
A 29-year-old man was admitted to this hospital because of anemia and jaundice.

The patient had been in his usual health until 4 days before admission, when increasing fatigue, malaise, headache, intermittent testicular discomfort, yellowed eyes, dark urine, nausea, and diffuse body aches developed and chronic leg pain worsened. No fever, rash, hematuria, dysuria, melena, or neurologic symptoms were present.

The patient had been generally well until 4 months earlier, when he traveled from an urban area of New England to his family's home in North Africa and stayed for 3 months. While there, a prolonged cough productive of green sputum developed, associated with fatigue, subjective fevers, chills, drenching night sweats, and intermittent dyspnea at rest, and he reportedly lost 9.1 kg. Ten days after his return to New England, and 20 days before this admission, he was seen in the medical clinic at this hospital.

On evaluation at that time, he appeared well. Weight was 92.7 kg, BMI 23.6, pulse 101 bpm; blood pressure and respirations were normal. Examination revealed mild acne, boggy and pale nasal turbinates with clear secretions, slight tonsillar enlargement, inspiratory crackles in the right lung base and expiratory wheezes in both apexes, and surgical scars on the right leg. Hemoglobin electrophoresis was normal, HIV testing was negative. A chest radiograph was normal. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test was negative.

The patient had a history of right leg fractures after a motor vehicle accident 3 years earlier requiring internal fixation, bone and skin grafting, with persistent chronic pain and venous swelling. One year before admission, after tibial surgery, hematocrit had decreased to 34.4% with MCV of 77 fl; the anemia resolved within 4 months. Medications included fluticasone and albuterol inhalers, ginkgo biloba, a multivitamin, and nicotine patches.

He had emigrated from North Africa to the United States 9 years earlier. He was a student, smoked marijuana and cigarettes, and reported no current alcohol or intravenous drug use. He was sexually active with his girlfriend. While visiting family in North Africa, his 14-year-old brother died of an unspecified disease. During that trip, he was exposed to sheep and drank unpasteurized cow's milk. His mother had osteopenia; his father and four other siblings were healthy.

On examination, blood pressure was 123/85 mm Hg, pulse 115; temperature, respirations, and oxygen saturation were normal. He appeared tired, with conjunctival icterus and jaundiced skin. The second heart sounds were loud without murmurs. Abdomen was soft with diffuse tenderness greatest in the right lower and left upper quadrants; testicles were diffusely tender without masses. Mild tenderness over the right knee and anterior shin with healed surgical scars.

Platelet count, white-cell differential, red-cell indexes (MCV, MCHC), coagulation tests, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, magnesium, total protein, albumin, globulin, alkaline phosphatase, ALT, AST, lipase, thyrotropin, folate, and vitamin B12 were normal. Thick and thin peripheral-blood smears and rapid malaria antigen testing were negative. HIV antibodies and heterophile antibodies were negative. Direct antiglobulin test (Coombs') was negative. Blood cultures were obtained. Urinalysis was normal; stool was guaiac-negative. ECG was normal. Chest radiograph showed clear lungs.

During the next 2 days, jaundice gradually improved. Blood cultures remained sterile. Ultrasound of the abdomen revealed mild hepatic steatosis; scrotal ultrasonography was normal.

presentation:
A 19-year-old woman was transferred to this hospital because of nausea, jaundice, and pruritus.

The patient had been well until approximately 2.5 weeks earlier, when malaise, vomiting, and upper respiratory symptoms developed, which resolved after 3 days. Approximately 1 week before admission, pruritus and dark urine developed. She noted darkening of her skin, which she attributed to sun exposure. Three days later, for a headache, she reportedly took three doses of oxycodone-acetaminophen received from a friend, pseudoephedrine, and tablets containing a combination of acetaminophen, aspirin, and caffeine.

Three days before admission, her mother brought her to the emergency department at another hospital because of increasing discoloration of her eyes and skin. She reported generalized malaise, decreased appetite, and a weight loss of approximately 3 kg during the previous month. She did not have diarrhea or abdominal pain. Vital signs were normal; conjunctivae and skin were icteric; remainder of examination was normal. Complete blood count, differential, renal function, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, alkaline phosphatase, and albumin were normal. Total protein was 8.2 g/dl (reference 6.0-8.0). She was advised to avoid alcohol and OTC medications and was discharged.

The next day, an episode of abdominal pain occurred. At follow-up the day after, she reported increasing pruritus and nausea. Testing for acute EBV infection was negative; CBC, differential, amylase, lipase, protein, and ammonia were normal. Urinalysis revealed orange, cloudy urine with 3+ bilirubin, 2+ blood, 1+ protein. Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis, thickened gallbladder wall, and normal-appearing liver and common bile duct. She was transferred to this hospital.

Three months earlier, the patient had given birth to a healthy child by spontaneous vaginal delivery after an uncomplicated full-term gestation. She breast-fed for 2 months. Prenatal testing for HBV, HCV, and HIV were reportedly negative. She had migraines treated with pseudoephedrine and acetaminophen-aspirin-caffeine as needed. She had had an abnormal Pap smear. Eighteen months earlier, she had undergone body piercing at a reputable shop; she had never received a blood transfusion. She had not recently traveled outside New England. She smoked cigarettes, drank alcohol rarely, and did not use intravenous drugs or eat wild mushrooms. No industrial exposures, recent sick contacts, or trauma. She was of mixed European ancestry. Her paternal grandmother had died of Laennec's cirrhosis; her paternal grandfather had died of lung cancer; maternal relatives had type 2 diabetes and asthma.

On examination, blood pressure was 143/83 mm Hg; other vital signs normal. Marked conjunctival icterus; skin was yellow without spider angiomas, palmar erythema, or telangiectasias. Abdomen was soft without tenderness, distention, or organomegaly. Neurologic examination revealed normal speech and attention and no asterixis.

CBC, differential, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, total protein, albumin, globulin, alkaline phosphatase, amylase, lipase, and ceruloplasmin were normal. EBV DNA PCR negative, CMV antigenemia negative, HIV and HCV antibodies negative, HAV IgM negative. Urine pregnancy test and toxicology screen were negative. Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis, gallbladder-wall thickening, no bile duct dilatation, unremarkable hepatic parenchyma, and patent hepatic vasculature.

presentation:
A 32-year-old woman, 3 weeks post partum, was seen in the emergency department of this hospital because of substernal chest pain.

The patient had been well until the day of admission when, while shopping at 7:25 PM, pain in the left jaw and substernal area developed suddenly. She drove home and called emergency medical services. During evaluation by EMS, oxygen was administered; her symptoms resolved after approximately 20 minutes. She declined further treatment. Shortly thereafter, the pain recurred, associated with shortness of breath.

On repeat evaluation at 8:34 PM, the patient rated the pain at 5 out of 10 and stated that at its onset, the pain rated 7. Blood pressure was 148/74 mm Hg. An ECG reportedly showed normal sinus rhythm at a rate of 90-100 bpm with ST-segment elevation of up to 4 mm in leads V3 through V6. Oxygen, aspirin, nitroglycerin, and morphine were administered. The pain resolved in the ambulance; a repeat ECG showed ST-segment elevations (1 to 4 mm) in leads V1 through V6, hyperacute T waves in V4-V6, and ST-segment depressions (1 to 1.5 mm) in the inferior leads. She arrived at the emergency department 1 hour 25 minutes after initial symptom onset.

The patient did not have diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, dyspnea, leg pain or swelling, or back pain. Three weeks earlier, she had had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39 weeks of gestation. Blood pressures during pregnancy ranged from 104-140 systolic and 60-82 diastolic across trimesters; no antihypertensive medications were given. The placenta weighed 340 g (less than the 5th percentile for gestational age); pathological examination revealed an increased amount of perivillous fibrin. Her child was healthy and breast-feeding.

During her first pregnancy, she had hypertension with onset before 20 weeks of gestation, followed by preeclamptic toxemia treated with magnesium sulfate. Between pregnancies, her blood pressure was normal and she took no medications. At the 10-day postpartum visit, she felt well; blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg.

She lived with her husband and her toddler and newborn and was a physician. She did not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Her parents had hypertension and hypercholesterolemia, and a distant cousin had had a stroke at a young age. She took no medications and had no allergies.

On examination, temperature was 36.2°C, blood pressure 143/92 mm Hg in the left arm and 137/81 mm Hg in the right arm, pulse 83 to 92 bpm without ectopy, respiratory rate 20, oxygen saturation 95% on ambient air and 100% on 2L nasal cannula. Carotid pulses were 2+ bilaterally without bruits; jugular veins were distended at 6-7 cm. The remainder of the examination was normal.

ECG in the ED showed sinus rhythm at 89 bpm with normal intervals, possible left atrial enlargement, incomplete right bundle-branch block, and ST-segment elevation of 1 mm in leads V2 and V3.

While in the emergency department, the patient continued to have episodes of substernal chest pain lasting approximately 5 minutes. Between episodes, the ST-segment changes resolved completely.

presentation:
A 25-year-old man was transferred to the emergency department of this hospital because of pain and swelling of the right hand and hypotension.

The patient had been well until 2 days earlier, when he noticed a lesion on the dorsum of his right hand, which he thought was an insect bite. The next day, the hand became swollen and painful, and he felt ill. In the evening, he had difficulty using his hand while working on a computer. The next morning, he had chills and his temperature rose to 38.6°C. At 5 PM, his mother found him obtunded, vomiting, and incontinent of feces and urine. His temperature was 38.6°C. He was taken to the emergency department of another hospital.

The patient appeared acutely ill and confused. Blood pressure was 73/25 mm Hg, temperature 37.9°C, pulse 145, respiratory rate 30, oxygen saturation 100% on 6L nasal cannula. The skin appeared cyanotic and was cool. The right hand was mottled and swollen, with a black eschar (1 cm) on the dorsum; the swelling extended up the proximal forearm. Capillary refill was 4 seconds. The patient could move his fingers but range of motion was limited. Blood cultures were obtained. ECG showed sinus tachycardia and nonspecific ST-T wave changes.

During 3.6 hours in the ED, 10 liters of crystalloid were infused, along with ceftriaxone, methylprednisolone, piperacillin-tazobactam, and norepinephrine to maintain MAP >65 mm Hg. The skin became less cyanotic with a flushed deep-red color; blue discoloration persisted over the right fingers distally. Urinary output improved. CT of the right hand showed soft-tissue swelling along the dorsum extending to the extensor tendons. No subcutaneous air, loculated fluid collection, intramuscular abscess, or bone destruction was seen. The patient was transferred to this hospital.

The patient had seasonal allergies. He was allergic to sulfamethoxazole (caused desquamation). He lived with his family on a farm in New England, worked in construction, and was exposed to horses, dogs, and cats. He hunted, most recently 7 months earlier. His mother reported that he had recently had abscesses on his left chin and buttocks treated with warm compresses. He had recently taken a course of prednisone for a persistent cough and took cetirizine as needed. He smoked cigarettes and drank alcohol socially; no IV drug use.

On examination at this hospital, the patient appeared ill and lethargic but was easily aroused and became oriented. Temperature was 37.3°C rising to 37.9°C within 20 minutes. Blood pressure 92/50 mm Hg, pulse 129, respiratory rate 28, oxygen saturation 96% on 100% nonrebreather. JVD was 10 cm at 45 degrees. Crackles in both lungs. There was a brown circular area of necrotic skin (2 cm) on the mid-dorsum of the right hand with two central pinpoint marks. The soft tissue of the hand and wrist was erythematous and swollen over palmar and dorsal surfaces to the proximal forearm, exquisitely tender on palpation. The fingers were held in flexion; sensation was intact in radial, ulnar, and median nerve distributions; radial and ulnar pulses were 2+. The patient was unable to move the right fingers more than 2-3 mm, and passive motion induced severe pain. No crepitation. Petechiae were present on both legs. A chest radiograph showed perihilar opacities and blurring of pulmonary vasculature consistent with interstitial edema.

Vancomycin and clindamycin were administered intravenously, and norepinephrine was continued.

presentation:
An unidentified black man who appeared to be approximately 40 years of age was brought to the emergency department of this hospital after suddenly losing consciousness. Witnesses had seen the patient jogging, and then they saw him suddenly stop, arch his back, and fall backward to the ground, striking the back of his head. Personnel from emergency medical services found him lying supine on the ground, unconscious, with stertorous respirations.

At the scene, the respiratory rate was 36, blood pressure 140/110 mm Hg, pulse 120. Blood glucose was 140 mg/dl. The man spontaneously flexed his right arm. Believing this was a seizure, the emergency medical technicians administered 10 mg of diazepam intravenously. A cervical collar was placed, and he was transported to this hospital.

On arrival, the patient was unresponsive to verbal and physical stimulation. Temperature was 37.6°C, blood pressure 116/71 mm Hg, pulse 100, respiratory rate 24. Oxygen saturation was 97% on 100% oxygen via bag-valve mask.

On examination, there was a 3 cm laceration along the left parietal occipital region of the head. Pupils were 4 mm, equal, round, and reactive to light. No facial trauma. No crepitus in the soft tissue of the neck; trachea midline. No carotid bruits. Examination of the chest, heart, and abdomen was normal. Rectal tone normal; stool guaiac-negative. Extremities were well perfused with normal pulses. No spinal deformities or evidence of back trauma.

On neurologic examination, the patient initially did not respond to voice stimulation or open his eyes spontaneously; shortly after arrival he began to respond to noxious stimuli. Bilateral corneal reflexes were present; face was symmetric. No nystagmus. Motor examination revealed withdrawal of all extremities from noxious stimuli. Reflexes were 2+ throughout; toes were turned down bilaterally.

An ECG obtained 20 minutes after arrival showed normal sinus rhythm, left ventricular hypertrophy, right bundle-branch block, and ST-segment depression in leads I, V4, V5, and V6, as well as T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4-V6.

CT of the brain and cervical spine showed no intracranial hemorrhage and no fracture of the skull or cervical spine. There was a subgaleal hematoma over the left parietal bone. CT of the abdomen showed a normal aorta with no injury to the liver, kidneys, or spleen.

Approximately three hours after arrival, the patient was identified by police and his wife was located. She reported that one year earlier the patient had collapsed while jogging and had been taken to another hospital. The patient, an engineer from Cameroon, had been told to stop playing soccer because he had a "thickened" heart, and a beta-blocker had been prescribed.
labs: On Presentation [Reference Range in brackets]: Complete blood count, electrolytes, glucose, coagulation tests, renal function, and liver function were all within normal ranges. Rapid qualitative creatine kinase test borderline positive. Rapid qualitative troponin T test negative. Toxicologic screening for alcohol, barbiturates, benzodiazepines, tricyclic antidepressants, opiates, and cocaine metabolites was negative.

presentation:
A 60-year-old man was evaluated in the clinic in mid-June because of fever, rigors, and sweats.

The patient had been well until 10 days earlier, when anorexia, a sore throat, diffuse myalgia, arthralgia, headache, malaise, and fever (temperature up to 39.3°C) developed, with frank rigors, night sweats, and dyspnea on climbing stairs. The sore throat had largely subsided by the time of examination. There was no cough, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, light stools, dysuria, or rash. His only medication was atorvastatin, and he was allergic to sulfonamides.

The temperature was 38.1°C. There was no rash, conjunctival injection, or scleral icterus. The mouth and throat were normal. The lungs were clear, and the heart sounds normal. The abdomen was unremarkable; the liver and spleen were not felt. There was no evidence of synovitis. The urine was cloudy and orange, positive for protein (+++), bilirubin (+), and trace-positive for glucose, ketones, and urobilinogen; sediment contained 20-50 white cells, 5-10 red cells, 3-5 granular casts, moderate squamous cells, and many bacteria per high-power field. Cultures of blood and urine were sterile, and a throat culture yielded no group A beta-hemolytic streptococci. A test for anti-deoxyribonuclease B antibodies was negative. An ELISA for antibodies against Borrelia burgdorferi was positive, but the test did not differentiate between acute and chronic infection.

During the next week, the patient's temperature ranged from 37.8°C to 39.4°C. Malaise developed, and the urine darkened. The patient lost 3.2 kg and continued to have dyspnea when climbing stairs. Chest radiographs showed a very small left pleural effusion or pleural thickening; the lungs and cardiomediastinal silhouette were unremarkable.

The patient resided with his wife and children in a wooded area north of Boston with a sedentary occupation in the same area. He had traveled to Cape Cod, Martha's Vineyard, and Nantucket, but not for the past several months, and had never been outside the United States. He had received several tick bites during the preceding two months. He owned a healthy dog that had never bitten him. There was no history of splenectomy, ingestion of raw meat, blood transfusions, adrenocorticosteroid use, or risk factors for HIV infection. He had not noticed any localized erythematous rash and was not aware of any exposure to rats or rabbits. No history of tobacco or alcohol use. He had had rigors on a daily basis during the week before the visit; during the paroxysms, his teeth chattered and he could not hold a glass of water without spilling it. He had not taken any antibiotics during the illness.

On examination by the infectious disease consultant, pulse was 95, respiratory rate 16. He was afebrile and appeared well. No petechiae, conjunctival injection, or lymphadenopathy. Neck was supple. Lungs clear, heart sounds normal. The liver descended 2 cm below the right costal margin; the spleen was not felt. Extremities appeared normal except for a few splinter hemorrhages and paleness of the hands. Western blot analysis revealed low levels of IgM, IgG, and IgA antibodies against B. burgdorferi, compatible with either current early infection or past treated infection.

presentation:
A 12-year-old girl was admitted to the hospital because of fever, vomiting, and an abrupt onset of unresponsiveness.

She had been well until one week earlier, when a cough, sore throat, and rhinorrhea developed. On the evening before admission, her temperature was 39.4°C. At noon on the following day, she began to vomit, and at 6:30 PM she was taken to the emergency department of another hospital. Temperature was 39.2°C, pulse 121, blood pressure 135/75 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation was 98% on ambient air. She was alert, fully oriented, able to walk, and able to talk coherently.

Approximately 75 minutes after arrival, flexion of her elbows and extension of her knees occurred, without opisthotonos. The patient began to stare straight ahead, became unresponsive to verbal stimulation, and was incontinent of urine. Painful stimulation provoked increased flexural posturing and moaning. Intravenous lorazepam (total 4 mg) was followed by relaxation of the posture, but the patient remained unresponsive. Phenobarbital (750 mg IV), thiopental, and succinylcholine were given, and the trachea was intubated with difficulty over 20 minutes. Oxygen saturation remained at 99-100% with bag-and-mask ventilation.

Vancomycin and ceftriaxone were administered. The patient was transferred by helicopter. Shortly before transfer, both pupils were 3 mm with sluggish reactivity. During the flight, the left pupil dilated to 7 mm and the right to 5 mm with sluggish light reactivity. The pulse rose to more than 200 bpm with increased blood pressure; adenosine was given with a slight, transient decrease in pulse. Twenty-four minutes into the flight, both pupils became fully dilated and unreactive. She arrived at this hospital and was admitted to the pediatric ICU.

The axillary temperature was 37.8°C. Oxygen saturation ranged from 85-89% on 100% oxygen. The patient was warm and well perfused. Pink, frothy secretions oozed from the endotracheal tube. She did not respond to noxious stimuli. Pupils were 7 mm and fixed. No corneal reflexes, no gag reflex. No spontaneous movement; muscle tone decreased. Reflexes and plantar responses were absent. Face was symmetric.

CT and CT angiogram of the head revealed diffuse cerebral and cerebellar edema with tonsillar and transtentorial herniation and markedly decreased arterial flow within higher-order branches of the circle of Willis from elevated intracranial pressure. No pachymeningeal or leptomeningeal enhancement. No acute infarction or hemorrhage. Chest radiograph showed dense bilateral air-space consolidation predominantly in upper lobes indicating pneumonia.

Arterial blood gas on 100% oxygen: PaO2 54 mm Hg, PaCO2 52 mm Hg, pH 7.19. MRI at 1:40 AM showed tonsillar herniation, obliteration of cisternal spaces, and near absence of flow in major intracranial arteries with evidence of brain ischemia on diffusion-weighted images. Cardiac ultrasound at 2:00 AM showed a dilated left ventricle with severe global dysfunction and a diffusely hypokinetic right ventricle.

An external ventricular drain was inserted; opening pressure was greater than 35 cm of water. Brain began to extrude through the drain. An intracranial-pressure bolt was placed; opening pressure was 80 cm of water. Hypotension developed. Neurologic examination at 5:00 AM revealed loss of brain-stem function. Several purpuric lesions, each less than 1 cm, were found on the ventral aspect of the right forearm. The patient died shortly after extubation.

presentation:
A 38-year-old woman was admitted to the hospital because of the acute onset of pain in the chest and upper back.

Two weeks before admission, the patient had given birth by cesarean section at eight and a half months' gestation to a healthy female infant. Early in the pregnancy, she had experienced chest pain. A cardiac ultrasonographic examination revealed mild aortic regurgitation, and a cardiac stress test was normal. She was gravida 11, para 3, having had seven first-trimester miscarriages. The delivery was complicated by blood loss and a drop in hematocrit to 17%; she did not receive a transfusion, and oral iron was begun. She was discharged one week after delivery.

One week after discharge, the patient awoke with "pressure" pain, maximal at onset, located initially in the throat, then radiating over the anterior chest and midway down her back. She had persistent neck pain with movement. The pain did not have a stabbing or burning quality and did not radiate to either arm or jaw. The pain was accompanied by mild dyspnea, wheezing, sweating, chilliness, and nausea. There was no vomiting, cough, fever, rash, arthralgia, headache, or claudication of the jaw, arms, or legs.

At the hospital where she had given birth, an ECG showed no abnormalities except sinus tachycardia. A CT scan of the thorax reportedly showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism but revealed inflammatory changes of the aortic arch, a small pericardial effusion, and a pulmonary artery diameter of 3.2 cm. She was transferred to this hospital.

The patient had a history of hypothyroidism, asthma, and gestational diabetes. No history of PE or DVT. Cholecystectomy 11 years prior; cesarean section 9 years prior for abruptio placentae. Medications included levothyroxine, insulin, albuterol, multivitamins, iron, and ascorbic acid. The levothyroxine dose had not been adjusted during pregnancy, and she had not regularly checked blood glucose. Allergic to penicillin and sulfonamides. Her father had hypertension and his first of several strokes at 35 years of age; he died of a stroke at 45. Her mother was well.

Temperature was 36.1°C, pulse 89, respiratory rate 24, blood pressure 140/65 mm Hg in both arms, oxygen saturation 100% on ambient air. She was pale, short, and overweight. No rash, lymphadenopathy, or splinter hemorrhages. JVP normal; thyroid not enlarged. Lungs clear. A decrescendo pandiastolic murmur, grade 3/4, was heard along the upper sternal area, and a grade 1 systolic murmur was present. Peripheral edema (+); pulses in arms and legs were ++ and equal bilaterally.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a tricuspid aortic valve without stenosis or vegetations. Mild-to-moderate aortic regurgitation with an eccentric jet angled at the anterior mitral leaflet. The aortic annulus and root were dilated: 40 mm at the sinuses of Valsalva, 42 mm at the sinotubular junction (normal <36), and 47 mm at the ascending aorta (normal <36). LV size and function were normal. Estimated RV systolic pressure was 45 mm Hg. Small pericardial effusion with diffuse fibrin deposition on the visceral pericardium.

CT of the chest showed a soft-tissue linear density within the mediastinum around the aortic root and ascending aorta, thought consistent with inflammation or hemorrhage. The ascending aorta was dilated with a maximal diameter of 4.5 cm. Small pericardial effusion. Small bilateral pleural effusions. No pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, or lymphadenopathy. The chest pain was unrelenting and was adequately relieved only by patient-controlled morphine analgesia.

presentation:
A 42-year-old woman was admitted to the emergency department of this hospital after cardiac arrest with pulseless electrical activity.

Approximately two weeks before admission, she had fractured her ankle when slipping on the ice. A closed reduction was performed, and a short leg cast was applied. On the evening of admission, the woman stood up from the couch, said she had chest pain and shortness of breath, and collapsed, apparently unconscious, with synchronous jerking of her arms and legs. Her husband called EMS; the paramedics found the woman on the floor, conscious, stating that she had chest pain, and somewhat combative. She rapidly became unresponsive with agonal respirations. The trachea was intubated. She subsequently became pulseless; two 1-mg doses of epinephrine and one 1-mg dose of atropine were administered. In transit, she required intermittent CPR for further episodes of pulselessness.

The patient had been well before the ankle fracture. One week before admission, her physician had indicated she had what the husband understood to be "varicose veins"; an ultrasound of the lower leg at another hospital was reported to be negative. She was taking hydrocodone-acetaminophen and ibuprofen as needed for ankle pain, as well as an oral contraceptive. Since the ultrasound, she had taken one baby aspirin daily.

She had no allergies. She had three young children. She worked as an administrative assistant. She had never smoked or drunk alcohol. Her mother had a history of phlebitis.

In the emergency department, the patient remained unresponsive. Temperature was 35.5°C, blood pressure 170/123 mm Hg, heart rate 154, oxygen saturation ranged from 78 to 85% on FiO2 1.0. No evidence of acute traumatic injury. Pupils 4 mm, sluggishly reactive. Trachea midline. Carotid pulses palpable. Heart and lungs normal on auscultation. Abdomen soft. Left lower leg in a cast to mid-calf. No swelling or edema of either leg. Skin was cool with peripheral cyanosis. No spontaneous movement and no gag reflex.

Almost immediately after arrival, she again became pulseless. After further CPR, the pulse returned. An ECG showed ventricular rate 56, AV dissociation, RBBB, and nonspecific ST-segment abnormalities. Chest radiograph showed diffuse infiltrate in the right upper lobe.

Blood pressure again dropped to 80/40 mm Hg, oxygen saturation dropped to 70-72%. A subsequent ECG showed ventricular rate 101, atrial fibrillation, incomplete RBBB, and nonspecific ST-T wave abnormalities. Bedside echocardiogram showed no pericardial tamponade but revealed possible RV dilatation. Additional episodes of pulselessness occurred requiring further CPR.

Thirty-five minutes after admission, alteplase (tPA) was given as a 15 mg IV bolus followed by 85 mg over 90 minutes. Pulselessness continued for about 20 minutes, then pulses returned, oxygen saturation improved to 80-90%, and systolic BP stabilized at 140 mm Hg. Pressors were discontinued. No more episodes of pulselessness. An echocardiogram showed marked RV enlargement and hypokinesis with moderate tricuspid regurgitation; LV size and function were normal. The patient was extubated later that day and mental status improved.

ground_truth: Cardiac arrest due to pulmonary embolism

presentation:
A 37-year-old man was admitted to this hospital because of muscle pain and weakness.

The patient had been well until the evening before admission, when mild diffuse myalgias developed. He awoke in the morning with diffuse muscle cramps and intense pain in his legs (rated 10 out of 10). On arising to go to the bathroom, he felt unsteady and had difficulty walking. After returning to bed, diffuse muscle pain persisted with weakness in arms and legs and numbness in his legs; he was unable to arise again.

He had had similar but much less severe pain intermittently for the past month, not associated with weakness and primarily in his upper thighs, after prolonged periods of rest. The pain occurred several times a week, most often at night, occasionally awakened him, and resolved spontaneously after a few minutes. He reported weight loss of 3.2 kg during the previous month, occasional blurred vision during the previous year, intermittent left wrist pain, and a slight tremor.

Six weeks before admission, he had fever, sore throat, and decreased appetite; a chest radiograph revealed patchy opacities in the right upper lobe consistent with possible pneumonia; symptoms improved after azithromycin. A diagnosis of gynecomastia had been made approximately 3 months earlier, when he presented with left breast tenderness and a palpable mass (4 cm by 3.5 cm) under the areola; mammography revealed bilateral subareolar densities consistent with gynecomastia. He also had androgenetic alopecia and seborrheic dermatitis. He had reportedly been treated for tuberculosis 17 years earlier.

His diet was high in carbohydrates; he reported eating 10 slices of pizza for dinner the night before admission. Medications included finasteride and clobetasol shampoo. He was born in Colombia, had lived in the United States for about 17 years, and had visited Colombia 6 months before admission. He drank alcohol infrequently, did not smoke or use illicit drugs, and reported no paint sniffing or toluene exposure. He worked in a restaurant. He had been sexually active with women only. His mother had diabetes and hypercholesterolemia and died of bladder cancer; his father had hypertension; a cousin and a niece had thyroid disease.

On examination, blood pressure was 166/72 mm Hg, pulse 100, temperature 37.3°C, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on ambient air, weight 66.2 kg. The patient was unable to stand. Strength in hip and knee flexion/extension was 3/5; ankles and elbows 4/5; hand grip 4+/5. Ankle, knee, and brachioradialis reflexes were absent. The remainder of the examination was normal.

CBC was normal, as were BUN, calcium, magnesium, total protein, albumin, globulin, bilirubin, creatine kinase, and AST. Serum toxicologic screening was negative. ECG showed sinus rhythm at 96 bpm with nonspecific ST-T wave changes. Urinalysis was normal.

Potassium chloride (120 mmol total) was administered orally and intravenously, with resolution of weakness.
