presentation:
A 50-year-old man was admitted to this hospital because of acute pain in the left flank.

The patient had been well until approximately 6 AM on the day of admission, when pain in the left flank occurred while he was bicycling. The pain was localized without radiation and increased during the next 7 hours from 3 to 8 out of 10. He came to the emergency department.

At a routine annual visit 4 days before admission, the patient reported feeling well. Examination revealed nontender varicoceles and prostatic hypertrophy; the remainder was normal. A tetanus-diphtheria-pertussis vaccine booster was administered. During the next 4 nights, he noted mild nausea and temperatures to 38.1°C without chills, which he attributed to the vaccination.

Fifteen years earlier, a diagnosis of Hodgkin's lymphoma had been made, and he received six courses of ABVD, radiation therapy, and splenectomy; hypothyroidism developed after treatment. He also had migraines, hyperlipidemia (type IIA), biapical pulmonary fibrosis, and pulmonary sarcoidosis. A stress echocardiogram 21 months before admission showed normal LV function at rest, a mildly thickened tricuspid aortic valve, mild aortic insufficiency, mild aortic-root dilatation, and trivial regurgitation of other valves; the stress test was negative for ischemia. Medications included calcium citrate, enteric-coated aspirin, simvastatin, levothyroxine, and ergocalciferol. He had received immunization against H. influenzae, pneumococcus, and meningococcus. He was married, worked in an office, exercised, drank alcohol in moderation, and did not smoke. His mother had breast cancer and coronary artery disease.

On examination, temperature was 37.3°C, blood pressure 158/72 mm Hg, pulse 76, respiratory rate 20, oxygen saturation 100% on ambient air. A holosystolic soft murmur, grade 2/6, was heard at the apex with radiation to the axilla, and a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur, grade 2/6, was heard at the base at the left upper sternal border. The abdomen was nondistended with moderate tenderness in the left flank below the costophrenic angle without guarding. Murphy's sign was negative. The remainder of the examination was normal.

Electrolytes, calcium, phosphorus, glucose, total protein, albumin, globulin, amylase, lipase, hematocrit, hemoglobin, platelet count, urinalysis, coagulation tests, renal function, and liver function were all normal.

CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed layered hyperdense material in the gallbladder (suggestive of gallstones) and a focal wedge-shaped defect in the left inferolateral kidney, with patent renal arteries and veins. CT of the thorax (PE protocol) showed bilateral posteromedial fibrosis and small stable pulmonary nodules in the right lung. No pulmonary artery filling defects and no lymphadenopathy.

Seven hours after presentation, temperature rose to 37.8°C.
labs: On Admission [Reference Range in brackets]: White-cell count 11,400/mm3 [4,500-11,000], Differential: Neutrophils 76% [40-70], Lymphocytes 14% [22-44], Monocytes 8% [4-11], Eosinophils 1% [0-8], Basophils 1% [0-3], D-dimer 663 ng/ml [<500], ESR 19 mm/hr [0-11], CRP 59.7 mg/L [<8.0], Lipoprotein A 11 mg/dl [<3].
ground_truth: Thromboembolic renal infarction due to endocarditis

presentation:
A 35-year-old woman was admitted to the hospital because of pain and swelling of the right thigh.

The patient had been in excellent health until the morning before admission, when she observed a "pimple" on her right thigh. During the course of the day, the lesion enlarged, with increasing pain, swelling, and erythema, and was accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and delirium. She was transported by air from the Caribbean and arrived at this hospital around midnight.

She was married and resided with her husband on a Caribbean island. She had participated extensively in scuba diving but had not done so in the preceding three weeks because of a "coral injury" to the lower leg. Her husband said she had had recurrent "strep throats" treated with antibiotics but had not had cellulitis. Her last menstrual period had occurred a few days before admission, and she used tampons. The household included a healthy pet dog. Several years earlier, she had lived in the Philippines. She did not smoke, rarely drank alcohol, and did not use illicit drugs; no recent exposure to ill persons or known insect bites.

Temperature was 37.5°C, pulse 128, respirations 20, blood pressure 85/60 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 98% on ambient air. The patient appeared ill and in pain. A small, indurated area of skin breakdown with surrounding erythema and warmth was present on the right thigh; no fluctuance was detected. She was unable to flex or extend the right hip because of pain and reported pain on passive extension of the right ankle. No lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear; heart normal except for sinus tachycardia. Abdomen unremarkable. Peripheral pulses intact. Sensation preserved.

Chest radiograph showed a large, ill-defined area of air-space disease in the left lung, probably representing pneumonia.

The temperature soon rose to 38.3°C, and blood pressure dropped to 70/40 mm Hg. Five liters of fluid and dopamine were given, and blood pressure rose to 100/80 mm Hg. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were administered.

CT of the thighs showed a diffuse, nonenhancing, honeycomb pattern within the subcutaneous tissue of the right thigh. Subcutaneous stranding and thickening of the skin were prominent in the posterolateral thigh; there was also thickening of the posterolateral deep fascia. Subfascial, high-density fluid surrounded the vastus lateralis, biceps femoris, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, gracilis, and vastus medialis muscles. The fluid extended along deep fascial planes from the lesser trochanter to the femoral supracondylar region. No subcutaneous or fascial gas. No walled-off collections.

The patient developed dyspnea. In the operating room, extensive cellulitis was nearly circumferential, extending from just distal to the inguinal ligament to above the knee. Bullae were observed on the posterior thigh proximal to the knee. A 10-cm incision showed considerable fluid within the subcutaneous fat without purulence. Muscle beneath the fascia appeared pink, not necrotic, and responded to electrocautery by contracting. No foul odor was detected.
labs: On Admission: Hematocrit 42.0%, White-cell count 5,900/mm3, Differential: Neutrophils 64%, Band forms 19%, Lymphocytes 7%, Atypical lymphocytes 1%, Monocytes 7%, Myelocytes 1%, Metamyelocytes 1%, Platelet count 251,000/mm3, PT 17.1 sec, aPTT 35.3 sec, D-dimer positive (0.5 to 2.0 μg/ml), Urea nitrogen 22 mg/dl, Creatinine 1.9 mg/dl, Glucose 174 mg/dl, Conjugated bilirubin 0.5 mg/dl, Total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dl, Sodium 137 mmol/L, Potassium 3.2 mmol/L, Chloride 107 mmol/L, Carbon dioxide 19.5 mmol/L, Magnesium 1.0 mmol/L, Creatine kinase 223 U/L. Calcium, phosphorus, fibrinogen, total protein, albumin, globulin, AST, CK isoenzymes, LDH, and alkaline phosphatase were normal.

presentation:
A 16-year-old boy was admitted to the hospital after being found unconscious in a snow bank at 6 AM on New Year's Day. He had been well until the night before admission, when he attended a party where alcohol was consumed. He was last seen at approximately 11 PM. Approximately 2 hours later, his friends and family noticed his absence and notified police; a search was begun. At approximately 6 AM, he was found unconscious in a snowbank by local firefighters and police officers. The ambient temperature was -15°C (5°F), with a wind-chill factor of approximately -29°C (-20°F). He was partially undressed, with his pants down and his right boot off; his limbs were buried in the snow, and a layer of ice surrounded his right foot.

On initial examination, he was drowsy and slow to respond but oriented, with spontaneous respirations and no shivering. The rectal temperature was 31.3°C (88.3°F), blood pressure 153/62 mm Hg (20 minutes later, 112/56), pulse 72 (20 minutes later, 52), respiratory rate 20, oxygen saturation 93% on ambient air. There were abrasions on his forehead. The distal right foot was encased in ice, both hands were cold and hard to palpation, and the left foot was cold but soft. The arms, buttocks, and legs (up to the thighs) were purplish-red, and the toes and fingers were blue. There were abrasions on the dorsal surfaces of the hands, legs, and feet. Neurologic examination showed no focal abnormalities.

Electrocardiography revealed sinus bradycardia of 59 bpm with sinus arrhythmia and a small Osborn wave (upward deflection at the junction of the QRS complex and the ST segment), features consistent with hypothermia.

Warm normal saline was infused. Hot blankets and forced-air warming were applied. The hands and right foot were placed in water at 40°C for active moist rewarming. Morphine was administered for pain.

The patient aroused after rewarming and reported having consumed alcohol at the party. He remembered leaving the party and attempting to walk home, falling repeatedly in the snow; he did not recall being assaulted. He had been healthy and had received all childhood immunizations. He was a high-school student living with his parents. He had smoked marijuana in the past and drank alcohol occasionally. No medications and no known allergies.

After 3 hours, rectal temperature was 37.3°C, blood pressure 143/56 mm Hg, pulse 81. He was awake and alert. Blood-filled bullae had developed on the hands and right foot, which were now soft but remained dusky and mottled. Left dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulses were palpable; right pedal pulses detected by Doppler only. No detectable blood flow to fingers or distal right foot. Hands and feet were anesthetic.
labs: On Presentation [Reference Range in brackets]: White-cell count 14,600/mm3 [4,500-13,000] (Neutrophils 81% [40-62], Lymphocytes 16% [27-40], Monocytes 3% [4-11]), Potassium 3.2 mmol/L [3.4-4.8], Carbon dioxide 18.3 mmol/L [23.0-31.9], Glucose 60 mg/dl [70-110], AST 58 U/L [10-40], Lipase 127 U/L [13-60], Amylase 206 U/L [3-100], Creatine kinase 3,815 U/L [60-400]. CBC remainder, renal function, calcium, phosphorus, bilirubin, ALT, alkaline phosphatase, total protein, albumin, and globulin were normal. Blood ethanol 1,172 mg/L [toxicity >1,000]; no other drugs detected.
